data.title: Woodside assumes control of Beaumont plant, delays lower-carbon ammonia production
data.description:
Woodside Energy (WDS) said Wednesday it assumed operational control of the Beaumont New Ammonia facility in Texas after completing ​performance testing and handover from OCI ​Global.

The company said the plant has the capacity to produce and ⁠export up to 1.1M metric tons/year ​of ammonia, adding diversity to its portfolio as it expands into new energy products.

Woodside (WDS) said ammonia production at the plant ​began in December, but production of ​lower-carbon ammonia is expected to be delayed until after 2026 due ‌to ⁠construction issues at a third-party feedstock supply facility; the company had planned to begin producing lower-carbon ammonia in the U.S. in this year's H2.

Woodside (WDS) acquired the Beaumont project in September 2024 for US$2.35B; the facility achieved a milestone in December, when it produced traditional, or gray, ammonia for the first time.

data.title: Celcuity outlines potential $2.5B annual peak revenue for gedatolisib amid pivotal clinical milestones and commercial launch preparations
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Celcuity Inc. (CELC) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO Brian Sullivan highlighted that 2025 was pivotal for Celcuity, stating the company is preparing for "the potential approval and commercialization of gedatolisib." He reviewed several milestones: "the FDA accepted our new drug application...granted it priority review with the Prescription Drug User Fee Act or PDUFA goal date of July 17, 2026," and noted the NDA was under the real-time oncology review program. Sullivan cited "unprecedented efficacy data from the PIK3CA wild-type cohort of the Phase III VIKTORIA-1 clinical trial," and expects reporting of the PIK3CA mutant cohort results in Q2 2026.

  • Sullivan also detailed late-breaking data presented at major oncology conferences and a recent peer-reviewed publication, emphasizing new benchmarks in progression-free survival (PFS) and duration of response for gedatolisib in HR-positive/HER2-negative advanced breast cancer. He recapped: "median progression-free survival...for the gedatolisib triplet...was 9.3 months compared to only 2 months for fulvestrant and the hazard ratio was 0.24."

  • Management discussed safety, noting "gedatolisib triplet was generally well tolerated...with mostly low-grade adverse events." Sullivan highlighted minimal impact on glucose levels, which distinguishes gedatolisib from other PI3K-alpha inhibitors.

  • Commercial preparations are advancing, with Sullivan stating, "we've since largely completed building the organization, including our sales force and internal systems required to operate as a commercial stage company." He estimated a "total addressable market for gedatolisib in the second-line setting is more than $5 billion," with potential to "generate peak revenue of up to $2.5 billion annually."

  • CFO Vicky Hahne reported, "our fourth quarter net loss was $51 million or $0.97 per share compared to $36.7 million net loss, or $0.85 per share, for the fourth quarter of 2024." Hahne added, "cash, cash equivalents and short-term investments were $441.5 million at the end of fiscal year 2025 and are expected to finance our operations through 2027."

Outlook

  • Management reiterated expectations for a "potential approval and commercialization of gedatolisib" with an FDA PDUFA goal date of July 17, 2026.

  • Sullivan projected reporting of the PIK3CA mutant cohort results from the VIKTORIA-1 trial in Q2 2026, with full results to be presented at a medical conference in 2026.

  • The company aims for "peak revenue of up to $2.5 billion annually" for a second-line indication if positive results are reported for both wild-type and mutant cohorts.

Financial Results

  • Hahne reported, "net loss for the full year was $177 million, or $3.79 per share, compared to $111.8 million, or $2.83 per share, compared to the same period in 2024."

  • Non-GAAP adjusted net loss for Q4 2025 was $38.4 million, or $0.73 per share. Research and development expenses for Q4 were $37.6 million, with a $4.1 million increase attributed to "increased employee and consulting expenses, of which $5.3 million related to commercial headcount additions and other launch-related activities."

  • General and administrative expenses for Q4 2025 were $11.6 million, up $8.6 million from the prior year period, driven largely by "increased employee and consulting expenses, of which $5.4 million related to noncash stock-based compensation."

  • Cash used in operating activities for Q4 2025 was $36.4 million, and for the year totaled $153.3 million.

Q&A

  • Maurice Raycroft, Jefferies LLC: Asked about the status update for the mutant data, specifically, "if the database lock is already in place." Sullivan replied, "No, I can't comment on that."

  • Tara Bancroft, TD Cowen: Inquired about feedback from physicians and launch segmentation. Sullivan stated, "we aren't going to be targeting or narrow casting our approach to doctors or patient segments."

  • Stephen Willey, Stifel: Asked about dose escalation for prostate cancer trials and efficacy metrics. Sullivan outlined, "we're using some standard methodology to stepwise increase the dose...we're in the midst of that. So I can't really comment on where we'll end up."

  • Gil Blum, Needham & Company: Questioned potential challenges with infusions. Sullivan noted, "the IV administration shows up as a negative factor in meaningfully less than 10% of the responses we have in this research."

  • Oliver McCammon, LifeSci Capital: Asked about learnings from inavolisib's launch. Sullivan said, "the label requires fairly extensive glucose monitoring" for that therapy, and emphasized gedatolisib's advantage in not inducing hypoglycemia.

  • Eva Fortea-Verdejo, Wells Fargo: Sought updates on European commercial strategy. Sullivan described plans for MAA submission after supplemental NDA completion and ongoing regulatory engagement in Europe and Japan.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts focused on data disclosure timelines, launch strategy, physician and payer feedback, and competitive positioning, with a neutral to slightly positive tone. Questions sought clarification rather than expressing skepticism.

  • Management maintained a confident and optimistic tone throughout, especially when discussing clinical data and commercial outlook. Sullivan frequently referenced positive feedback, stating, "the feedback we've received, again, is very positive on that front as well."

  • Compared to the previous quarter, both analysts and management remained consistent in tone; however, management displayed increased confidence as regulatory milestones and commercial preparations advanced.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance language remained focused on regulatory and commercial milestones, with the current quarter specifying the PDUFA date and expected timing for mutant cohort data.

  • Strategic focus shifted toward final launch preparations and commercial execution, while clinical milestone reporting timelines became more concrete.

  • The analysts' focus transitioned from clinical data and regulatory process (Q3) to commercialization, payer feedback, and competitive landscape (Q4).

  • Key financial metrics such as net loss and operating expenses increased compared to Q3, reflecting ramp-up of commercial activities and expanded headcount.

  • Management maintained a confident tone, building on strengthening market and regulatory position, with analysts' sentiment stable and focused on upcoming catalysts.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited the inability to discuss ongoing trial progress for the PIK3CA mutant cohort as a limitation.

  • Potential challenges with infusion administration were acknowledged but downplayed based on market research.

  • The competitive environment was addressed, with Sullivan indicating that alternative PI3K inhibitors may face biological and safety limitations compared to gedatolisib.

  • No issues were reported regarding operational runway, with cash reserves expected to finance the company through 2027.

Final Takeaway

Celcuity's leadership underscored 2025 as a transformative year, marked by the NDA acceptance for gedatolisib, priority review, and robust clinical data supporting its potential as a new standard of care in HR-positive/HER2-negative advanced breast cancer. The company is advancing commercial launch preparations, targeting a $5 billion market and projecting up to $2.5 billion in annual peak revenue, while maintaining strong cash reserves and a confident outlook as it approaches pivotal regulatory and clinical milestones in 2026.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Golar LNG launches strategic review, taps Goldman Sachs as advisor
data.description:
Golar LNG (GLNG) up 5.9% post-market Wednesday after saying it has initiated a formal process to evaluate strategic alternatives, including a potential sale of the company, and appointed Goldman Sachs as a financial advisor.

Golar (GLNG) said the review aims to accelerate the FLNG growth pipeline and maximize shareholder value, reflecting its "successful transition into a high-growth, pure-play floating liquefied natural gas company."

The review will include a comprehensive evaluation of the company's platform, including its FLNG technology, contract backlog, and growth pipeline, and will explore potential alternatives including a sale of the company, a merger or other business combination, divestiture of assets, or further optimization of the corporate structure.

data.title: MillerKnoll outlines Q4 net sales outlook of $955M–$995M while expanding retail footprint and managing Middle East risks
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: MillerKnoll, Inc. (MLKN) Q3 2026

Management View

  • Andrea Owen, President and CEO, highlighted "continued sales and order growth and disciplined execution" despite ongoing macroeconomic and geopolitical uncertainty and severe weather impacts. Owen emphasized the 30th anniversary of MKPS, a manufacturing system developed in partnership with Toyota, calling it "a significant competitive advantage" for efficient and high-quality production.
  • Owen stated, "We expect to continue to fully offset tariff costs for the remainder of this fiscal year as we did in the third quarter." She also noted disruption in the Middle East, saying, "we do expect some impact to fourth quarter sales and costs."
  • North America Contract showed "order growth in most sectors" and Owen expressed optimism about demand resiliency. Owen discussed upcoming launches at the NeoCon trade show, including products from Herman Miller, Knoll, Geiger, NaughtOne, HAY, Muuto, and Maharam.
  • In Global Retail, Owen reported "segment comparable sales increased 5.5%" with North America up 3.9%, despite adverse weather. The company opened new DWR locations in Fort Worth and Pittsburgh and a Herman Miller store in Phoenix, planning to open 3 to 4 more locations before fiscal year-end, aiming to "approximately double our DWR Herman Miller store footprint over the next several years."
  • Owen welcomed Claire Spofford, former CEO of J. Jill, to the Board, citing her "consumer insight, retail strategy and governance experience."
  • CFO Kevin Veltman stated, "In the third quarter, we generated adjusted earnings per share of $0.43 compared to $0.44 in the same quarter last year... Consolidated net sales for the quarter were $927 million, up 5.8% year-over-year on a reported basis and 3.8% higher organically."
  • Veltman reported, "We generated $61 million in cash flow from operations in the quarter and reduced our debt by $41 million, lowering our debt-to-EBITDA ratio to 2.75x as defined by our lending agreement."

Outlook

  • Veltman provided Q4 guidance: "We expect net sales to range between $955 million and $995 million, up 1.4% versus prior year, at the midpoint of $975 million."
  • "Gross margin is projected to be between 37.5% and 38.5% and includes higher expected logistics costs from higher oil prices related to the conflict in the Middle East."
  • Adjusted diluted earnings are expected to range between $0.49 and $0.55 per share, including "our current estimate that the direct impact of the Middle East conflict will be $8 million to $9 million in the quarter or $0.09 to $0.10 per share."
  • Incremental year-over-year operating expense for new store locations in global retail is expected at $3.5 million to $4.5 million.

Financial Results

  • Consolidated orders grew to $932 million, up 9.2% as reported.
  • The consolidated backlog was $712 million at quarter end.
  • North America Contract net sales were $489 million; orders increased to $491 million. Operating margin for the segment was 8.6% and adjusted margin was 9.8%.
  • International Contract net sales reached $157 million. Orders were $160 million, with adjusted operating margin at 8.2%.
  • Global Retail net sales were $281 million, with a segment operating margin of 2.2% and adjusted margin of 2.8%.
  • Dividend declared at $0.1875 per share, payable April 15.

Q&A

  • Douglas Lane, Water Tower Research LLC, asked about weather impact. Owen responded, "We definitely saw lower traffic than normal across our retail stores. We had quite a few closures during that frigid weather period."
  • Lane questioned cost pressures from the Middle East. Owen replied, "We haven't seen much except for increase in diesel and things that are really impacting oil-related fuel so far, but we anticipate we'll see increase in cost of plastics, foam..."
  • Lane inquired about inventory strategy. Owen stated, "We have dual supply in a variety of places for most of our really important components... so far, not yet."
  • Lane asked about office environment trends. Owen said, "We continue to see momentum. We continue to see architectural billings moving in the right direction."
  • Olivia May Witte, William Blair, questioned contract and retail volatility. Owen explained, "We have built in some caution around oil prices... so far, we have a consumer that tends to be premium and tends to be rather unaffected by many of these changes."
  • Witte also asked about government contract trends. John Michael, President of North America Contract, said, "We came into this year expecting that the federal government business would be rather tough... but there's still activity there."
  • Reuben Garner, The Benchmark Company, clarified the Middle East impact. Veltman responded, "The sales that we don't expect to be able to ship in the quarter is pretty close to what our run rate has been, that $12 million that I mentioned."
  • Garner asked about AI and tech sector demand. Michael stated, "the tech sector is very active right now, particularly in the Bay Area."
  • Gregory Burns, Sidoti & Company, asked about store opening pace. Veltman replied, "we're expecting next year's store openings to be in a similar zone to the 14 to 15 this year, maybe a touch higher."
  • Burns asked about retail gross margin. Propst explained, "we had a favorable freight true-up last year" and "incremental ship revenue costs in Q3 as we pushed into some free shipping promos."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts focused on macro risks, cost pressures, and retail momentum with a slightly cautious tone, highlighted by repeated questions on Middle East impacts and consumer demand.
  • Management maintained a confident and proactive stance, repeatedly emphasizing scenario planning and flexibility. Owen used phrases such as "we anticipate," "we are very experienced in navigating tariff changes," and "we continue to be optimistic."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management's tone in prepared remarks was similarly confident, but Q&A responses reflected more caution regarding global risks and cost inflation.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Q3 guidance incorporated explicit headwinds from the Middle East conflict, while Q2 focused more on tariff risks and new store investments.
  • Strategic focus shifted from primarily execution and expansion to also include risk mitigation and supply chain agility.
  • Analysts in both quarters queried about macro impacts, but Q3 saw heightened concern on geopolitical and oil-related risks.
  • Gross margin projections narrowed slightly, reflecting expected logistics and commodity pressures.
  • Management's confidence in retail expansion and contract order growth remained steady, but there was increased emphasis on scenario planning.

Risks and Concerns

  • Ongoing macroeconomic and geopolitical uncertainty, especially the Middle East conflict, is expected to impact Q4 sales and costs.
  • Management flagged potential cost increases in oil, logistics, plastics, and foam, and is monitoring these developments daily.
  • Severe weather disrupted retail traffic and operations.
  • The government contract business is "rather tough" with expectations for choppiness in coming months.

Final Takeaway

MillerKnoll delivered a solid quarter, demonstrating resilience in sales and order growth despite challenging external conditions. Management anticipates continued headwinds from the Middle East conflict and higher oil-related costs but remains committed to expanding its retail footprint, leveraging its manufacturing efficiency, and pursuing growth in both contract and retail segments. The company is closely monitoring cost pressures and stands ready to adjust pricing and supply strategies as needed, while maintaining a disciplined approach to capital allocation and shareholder returns.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Dolphin Entertainment targets accelerated margin expansion and cash flow growth with DealMaker and AI initiatives
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Dolphin Entertainment (DLPN) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO William O'Dowd highlighted that 2025 was "the first without a major acquisition," emphasizing the company's transition from an acquisition-driven strategy to focusing on organic growth and operational leverage. O'Dowd reported, "Full year revenue grew approximately 10% to $56.7 million. Fourth quarter revenue was $15.6 million, up 27% year-over-year." He stressed that this growth was "entirely organic" and pointed to a significant acceleration in profitability and cash flow. "Full year adjusted EBITDA reached $2.9 million, which is up over 200% from $900,000 in 2024." O'Dowd underscored the company's capital-light model, noting "Dolphin requires very little capital expenditure to operate...when we generate incremental EBITDA, that incremental EBITDA translates almost directly into free cash flow." He cited $127 million in net operating loss carryforwards as providing substantial tax shielding.
  • On strategic initiatives, O'Dowd described the "strategic partnership with DealMaker" as "one of the most exciting developments in Dolphin's history and a meaningful growth catalyst for us," targeting celebrity and influencer-led capital raises with minimal capital outlay.
  • O'Dowd also introduced "Dolphin Intelligence," a new division focused on AI-driven marketing, which "offers a suite of new services, including generative engine optimization and AI engine optimization strategy." The division is led by Mark Anderson and is positioned as both a growth driver and an internal efficiency tool.
  • CFO Mirta Negrini stated, "Total revenue for the year ended December 31, 2025, was $56.7 million, an increase of 10% from $51.7 million in the prior year." Negrini reported, "Operating loss was $39,058 for the year ended December 31, 2025, compared to an operating loss of $10.5 million for the year ended December 31, 2024." She added, "Net loss for 2025 was approximately $3.1 million...This compares to a net loss of $12.6 million in 2024."

Outlook

  • O'Dowd projected "continued revenue growth in 2026" with contributions from both "dealmaker-related marketing engagements and Dolphin Intelligence services as those ramp in the second half of the year." He stated, "We expect adjusted EBITDA margin expansion to continue. At 5% adjusted EBITDA margin in 2025, we believe we are just getting started. The infrastructure is built and incremental revenue carries high flow-through." He also reiterated the expectation that "adjusted EBITDA to grow significantly faster than revenue again in 2026."
  • O'Dowd provided visibility on cash flow drivers: "approximately $1 million in expected annualized lease savings beginning at the end of this year when our current New York leases roll off. Los Angeles ends at the end of next year in 2027." He also pointed to "full repayment of our bank debt within approximately 2.5 years, September 29, 2028."

Financial Results

  • Dolphin reported full year revenue of $56.7 million and Q4 revenue of $15.6 million. Adjusted EBITDA for the full year was $2.9 million, with Q4 adjusted EBITDA at $1.7 million, compared to an adjusted EBITDA loss of $0.5 million in Q4 2024. Operating expenses for 2025 were $56.7 million, including $2.4 million of depreciation and amortization. Net loss for 2025 was $3.1 million, down from $12.6 million in 2024. Basic and diluted loss per share for the year was $0.27.
  • The company highlighted $127 million in federal and state NOLs as a major factor expected to shield cash taxes and support free cash flow conversion.

Q&A

  • Derek Greenberg, Maxim Group LLC: "I wanted to ask about just the DealMaker partnership you had outlined...if you could get a little more granular in terms of how that's tied directly to deal flow and how those opportunities will be sourced?" O'Dowd responded that DealMaker allows Dolphin "to scale a massive opportunity...to launch consumer products with our own clients" and described anticipated marketing campaigns as "in the 6 figures per year per brand." He noted a "very strong and robust pipeline" from both Hollywood partners and existing clients.
  • Greenberg: "Generally, what do you expect the length of deals to be? And who's typically the investors...?" O'Dowd explained, "Reg CF allows you to raise up to $5 million per raise every 12 months...we would look to complete the raise in full within 4 months typically." He added, "the typical investment size is probably $1,000 to $2,000" per investor, highlighting the community-driven nature of the offerings.
  • Greenberg: "Just your venture portfolio...timing on your end in terms of when you think to add additional ventures...?" O'Dowd stated, "We had given ourselves a 60-day window...I would hope to be in market with the first one this summer."
  • Greenberg: "AI and Intelligence segment...how you expect that to contribute to growth...?" O'Dowd detailed that existing clients are expected to be early adopters and that Dolphin Intelligence is also "a business development tool for us" to attract new, larger clients.
  • Greenberg: "Youngblood...biggest opportunity is up ahead with selling the streaming rights. How is that process going...?" O'Dowd said, "we have a second window...which is what we call electronic sell-through or pay-per-view...That window is opening up here at the end of the month of March."
  • Greenberg: "Overall performance this year, double-digit organic growth. Do you think that level is sustainable going forward?" O'Dowd replied, "I do believe we're going to grow every year just organically like this...any incremental revenue growth should have an outsized importance on the margin expansion."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts demonstrated a positive and inquisitive tone, expressing interest in the specifics of the DealMaker partnership, Dolphin Intelligence, and the sustainability of growth, as seen in questions like, "Do you think that level is sustainable going forward?"
  • Management's tone was confident and focused, using phrases such as "we are just getting started" and "we believe...2026 will continue the beginning of that inflection." There was a consistent emphasis on operational leverage and strategic opportunities.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management's confidence level remained high, but the current call featured more forward-looking detail about new business lines and specific cash flow catalysts. Analysts' tone shifted from congratulatory (Q3) to more probing about growth drivers and execution details in Q4.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance language in Q4 emphasized continued revenue growth and expanding EBITDA margins, building on the Q3 narrative of strong organic growth and operational simplification.
  • Strategic focus shifted in Q4 to highlight the launch of Dolphin Intelligence and the DealMaker partnership, whereas Q3 focused more on agency cross-selling and operational results.
  • Analysts in Q4 directed more of their questions toward new business development and monetization of strategic initiatives, reflecting heightened interest in forward-looking catalysts.
  • Management’s tone in both quarters was highly confident, but Q4 included more detailed discussion of pipeline development, margin expansion, and cash flow optimization.
  • Key metrics such as adjusted EBITDA and net loss showed marked improvement in Q4 versus Q3, and there was a greater emphasis on the expected acceleration of free cash flow in the current quarter.

Risks and Concerns

  • O'Dowd acknowledged that "our business has seasonality to it. Historically, our first quarter tends to be our lightest with revenue building through the year so that the fourth quarter typically becomes our strongest."
  • The CEO flagged that the theatrical window for Youngblood "may have underperformed, but we are straightforward about that. The real opportunity has always been in the streaming and digital distribution tail."
  • Management stressed a disciplined, capital-light approach to new ventures, minimizing financial risk: "We contribute our capabilities rather than our capital, and we look for opportunities with asymmetric upside."

Final Takeaway

Dolphin Entertainment’s management presented a narrative of accelerating organic growth, margin expansion, and a capital-light business model backed by significant tax assets. The company is leveraging new strategic partnerships, particularly with DealMaker and the launch of Dolphin Intelligence, to expand its addressable market and drive recurring, high-margin revenue streams. Management conveyed confidence in further EBITDA and cash flow growth through 2026, supported by cost-saving catalysts and operational scale, while maintaining a disciplined approach to risk and capital allocation.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Planet 13 signals margin recovery and positive cash flow focus with California exit and Florida expansion
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Planet 13 Holdings Inc. (PLNH) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Larry Scheffler, Co-Chairman & Co-CEO, reported that "Q4 was a better quarter where the work we've been doing begin to show up in the results" and pointed out stabilization across operations. He noted the SuperStore, including DAZED!, generated $9.2 million, which was "essentially flat from Q3" despite a 6.3% year-over-year decline in Las Vegas visitor volume and a 15.6% drop in average visitor spending downtown. Scheffler highlighted that the F1 race in November displaced approximately 4 days of retail traffic, yet revenue was flat, suggesting a resilient underlying business run rate. He emphasized that the foundation heading into 2026 is stronger, with neighborhood stores recording $14 million, including a one-time benefit from a loyalty accrual adjustment in Florida.
  • Steve McLean, Interim CFO, stated, "In Q4, Planet 13 generated $25.2 million in total revenue compared to $23.3 million in Q3, sequential growth of approximately 8%." He flagged that the California divestiture will remove $2.5 million to $3 million in quarterly revenue going forward but will be offset by margin and cash flow improvements. McLean noted gross profit of $11.2 million with a 44.6% gross margin, a recovery from Q3's 21.3% margin, which was impacted by a $3.5 million inventory reserve in Florida. Adjusted EBITDA improved, narrowing from a $4.1 million loss in Q3 to a $0.3 million loss in Q4. He concluded, "The California exit is a meaningful step in the right direction here."
  • Robert Groesbeck, Co-Chairman & Co-CEO, explained that 2025 focused on "deliberate repositioning, exiting markets that were consuming capital without a credible path to profitability, strengthening our Florida foundation and bringing the cost structure in line with the realities of today's cannabis market." He detailed structural changes in Nevada, including closing two cultivation facilities and consolidating production at Bell Drive, aiming to remove a persistent drag on profitability. Groesbeck also spotlighted new Clark County regulations restricting unlicensed hemp retailers, describing it as "a meaningful step toward restoring the supply and demand balance this market desperately needs."

Outlook

  • Management stated that the priority for 2026 is to "reach positive cash flow, demonstrate the earnings power of this portfolio and deliver on the commitment we've made to our shareholders." Groesbeck indicated that Q2 2026 will be the first quarter reflecting the repositioned portfolio, with no California drag and improving Florida productivity. McLean noted that with the BHO lab complete and no major CapEx anticipated, the company expects its cash position to improve meaningfully throughout 2026.

Financial Results

  • The company reported $25.2 million in total revenue, up from $23.3 million in Q3, and $11.2 million in gross profit with a 44.6% gross margin. Q3's margin was 21.3%, affected by a $3.5 million inventory reserve in Florida. Sales and marketing expense declined 5% sequentially to $1.1 million, and G&A was flat at $12 million. Adjusted EBITDA improved to a $0.3 million loss from a $4.1 million loss in Q3. Cash and restricted cash ended Q4 at $15.6 million. The California divestiture is expected to remove $2.5 million to $3 million in quarterly revenue but improve margin and cash flow.

Q&A

  • Kenric Tyghe, Canaccord Genuity: Asked about confidence and timing for BHO lab approval in Florida and materiality to outlook. Groesbeck responded, "We've literally submitted every single document required for approval. We've met every requirement of the OMMU...we're currently optimistic that we've had multiple delays."
  • Steve McLean added that Florida is already cash flow positive and expects continued improvement, with the BHO lab and licensing partnerships set to bring more products online.
  • Pablo Zuanic, Zuanic & Associates: Inquired about SKU expansion in Florida and store growth. Groesbeck could not specify partner details but said, "We've got a number of approvals pending with the OMMU now on different product varieties and SKUs...we should have some announcements out to the market here shortly." McLean confirmed two new Florida stores are opening soon, with further expansion on a "wait and see" basis.
  • Brenna Cunnington, ATB Cormark Capital Markets: Asked about DAZED! performance and Nevada margin outlook. McLean reported DAZED! saw "about 25% plus revenue increase versus last year" and expects margins "north of 50% starting in the first quarter" post-California exit, with positive EBITDA anticipated for the full year beyond Q1.
  • Kenric Tyghe questioned the impact of the hemp ban. Scheffler estimated, "We're predicting $1 million to $2 million per month we lost to the hemp stores on the strip...we anticipate a huge increase in revenue for us starting in 2026, the second half."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts maintained a cautiously optimistic tone, seeking clarity on timelines for regulatory approvals, margin expansion, and new market opportunities. Questions were probing but not confrontational, with a constructive focus on operational execution and strategic drivers.
  • Management expressed measured optimism, emphasizing operational stabilization, margin recovery, and readiness for regulatory catalysts. Confidence increased from Q3, with more specific forward guidance and clear articulation of structural improvements. Phrases like "the trajectory is clear" and "we are ready to move the moment we get the green light" signaled conviction.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, sentiment shifted from defensive explanations of headwinds to proactive communication about margin recovery, cost discipline, and strategic progress.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The Q4 call reflected a more positive trajectory, with management highlighting sequential revenue growth, gross margin recovery, and substantial narrowing of the adjusted EBITDA loss. In Q3, discussions were dominated by headwinds, inventory write-downs, and the drag from California. By Q4, the narrative shifted to the benefits of the California exit, stabilization in Florida, and anticipation of regulatory and market tailwinds in Nevada. Analysts in both quarters pressed for clarity on regulatory timing and operational improvements, but Q4 featured more forward-looking, less defensive discourse from management.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited ongoing macro headwinds in Las Vegas, including reduced visitor volume and spending. The timing of BHO lab approval in Florida remains uncertain due to regulatory delays. The Supreme Court’s stance on Florida’s adult-use ballot initiative was viewed as unfavorable, potentially delaying market expansion. The impact of the California divestiture includes a top-line revenue reduction, but this is expected to be offset by improved margins and cash flow. Management acknowledged continued pressure from illicit competition but cited new regulations as a potential inflection point.

Final Takeaway

Leadership emphasized that Planet 13 has executed a deliberate repositioning, with the exit from California and operational changes in Nevada setting the stage for meaningful margin expansion and positive cash flow in 2026. The company expects these structural moves, combined with new regulatory support in Nevada and a strengthened Florida footprint, to deliver visible improvements in profitability and shareholder value as early as the second quarter of 2026.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Marchex anticipates 10% revenue growth run rate in 2026 while accelerating AI-driven bundled solutions
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Marchex, Inc. (MCHX) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Executive Chairman Russell Horowitz stated that Marchex is at "a very positive inflection point, both strategically and operationally" and highlighted the company's advancement in expanding its customer footprint, evolving product and technology capabilities, and creating sales momentum.
  • Horowitz described a shift toward delivering "comprehensive solutions that address high-value impact needs across the entire customer acquisition and optimization journey" by leveraging AI-driven strategic insights and introducing bundled solutions across the customer value chain.
  • Horowitz also emphasized Marchex's positioning as an "AI beneficiary," with rapid development and deployment of new products leveraging AI for customer acquisition and retention. He noted ongoing trials and early adoption of agentic workflows and bundled solutions on the Engage platform.
  • President & Chief Revenue Officer Troy Hartless reported the "primary completion of our technology platform migration by the end of the year," involving the migration of approximately 1,000 customers. Hartless indicated the company is now "well positioned to focus on accelerating our revenue growth and delivering margin expansion during 2026." He mentioned the launch of a unified user interface, new vertical AI capabilities, and a collaboration with Archenia to develop and sell combined products such as conversational AI agents and AI-verified outcomes.
  • Hartless said, "we have had initial positive indications of adoption of the combined solutions for Marchex's existing customers in the Home Services and Auto Services verticals."
  • CFO Brian Nagle reported, "Revenue for the fourth quarter of 2025 was $10.8 million, which is down from $11.5 million for the third quarter of 2025." Nagle noted the decrease was due to migration activities but stated, "we saw favorable impact of new sales and existing customer up-sells benefit the company in the quarter."
  • Nagle highlighted operating efficiencies, a lower cost structure, and anticipated improved gross profit margins. He stated, "cash decreased to $9.9 million from $10.3 million at the end of the third quarter of 2025."
  • COO Francis Feeney provided details on the pending Archenia acquisition, indicating the closing is anticipated for June 2026, subject to approval and required audits. Feeney outlined that the combined company could see quarterly revenue run rates of approximately $15 million, with potential annualized growth of 15% to 20% in 2026.

Outlook

  • Nagle indicated first quarter 2026 revenue is expected to be in the range of fourth quarter 2025 levels, with adjusted EBITDA of $500,000 or more.
  • For the second quarter of 2026, Nagle projected sequential revenue growth and adjusted EBITDA "potentially increasing to more than $1 million."
  • Nagle stated, "we can see sequential quarterly revenue increases during 2026 and that over the course of the year, we can see revenue growth on a run rate basis in the 10% range from 2025 year-end levels." He also expects adjusted EBITDA margins of 10% or more.
  • Feeney explained that the combined company, if the Archenia acquisition closes, "could grow in the 15% to 20% range in the course of '26."

Financial Results

  • Nagle reported, "Revenue for the fourth quarter of 2025 was $10.8 million, which is down from $11.5 million for the third quarter of 2025."
  • Cash decreased to $9.9 million from $10.3 million at the end of Q3 2025 due to timing of customer payments.
  • The company cited operating expenditure efficiencies and a lower overall cost structure.

Q&A

  • Ross Koller, Koller Capital, asked about the progress of joint selling efforts with Archenia. Horowitz replied, "so far, the joint sales calls have been very positive and very much strategically operational...we actually already have multiple orders in hand from the installed customer base for these new products."
  • Koller inquired about the addressable opportunity within the installed base. Horowitz responded, "our top 50 customers represent about 80% of our revenue...we believe that they are very relevant and very applicable to the vast majority of those top 50 as well as other customers beyond the top 50."
  • Koller asked about investor relations strategy and stock valuation. Horowitz stated, "we feel the current stock price doesn't reflect our value or even the incremental value we believe we're in the process of both creating and validating" and shared that Marchex hired a new IR firm to "get a lot more active in reaching out to new investors."
  • Vijay Devar, Northland Capital Markets, asked if bookings grew sequentially and year-on-year. Horowitz replied, "bookings were similar to the prior quarter...we see accelerations of bookings as we're ending Q1 and going into Q2 in a way that we think can potentially meaningfully move the math."
  • Devar asked about call volumes. Horowitz said, "call volumes have been relatively consistent...the primary variables are customer expansion, up-selling the new products and getting the benefits or stacking effect of what we're starting to see unfold."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts focused on growth from joint solutions, the size of the installed base opportunity, and the company's communication strategy, with questions indicating cautious optimism and a desire for tangible progress.
  • Management maintained a positive and confident tone throughout, particularly emphasizing inflection points and runway for growth, stating "we think we're at an inflection point" and "the real catalyst that we see is with these products that really unlock the strategic insights into action and outcome-based products."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, both management and analysts maintained a slightly more optimistic tone, with management expressing greater confidence in the company's trajectory and the strategic benefits of the Archenia transaction.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance language remained focused on run rate revenue growth in the 10% range and adjusted EBITDA margins of 10% or more, consistent with the previous quarter, but with additional detail on quarterly sequential growth and specific EBITDA targets.
  • Strategic focus shifted from completing the technology migration to leveraging the new platform for product innovation and bundled AI solutions.
  • Analysts' questions in both quarters centered on addressable market size and growth prospects, but current quarter exchanges reflected heightened interest in early results from joint Archenia-Marchex solutions and investor outreach.
  • The company's tone evolved from cautious optimism to a more assertively positive stance, highlighting the positive initial sales impact of new offerings and more active investor communications.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management mentioned revenue dilution effects from the technology migration but indicated these are now largely behind the company.
  • The outcome of the Archenia acquisition remains uncertain, with closing subject to audited financials, fairness opinions, and shareholder approval, anticipated by June 2026.
  • Horowitz acknowledged that "the current stock price doesn't reflect our value" and that market recognition will depend on delivering results.
  • No additional significant risks or challenges were raised by analysts during Q&A.

Final Takeaway

Marchex emphasized that it is entering 2026 with a completed technology migration, a focus on accelerating revenue growth, and the ongoing integration of AI-driven bundled solutions. Management highlighted strong initial adoption of new products, reinforced its 10% run rate revenue growth target, and pointed to the potential strategic impact of the Archenia acquisition. The company remains confident in its ability to deliver sequential revenue and margin expansion, with a clear mandate to communicate progress and value to investors in the months ahead.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Noodles & Company outlines 2026 revenue target of up to $493M with comp sales growth of 6% to 9% amid portfolio optimization
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Noodles & Company (NDLS) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO Joseph Christina described 2025 as a pivotal year for the company, highlighting “system-wide comp sales growth of nearly 7% in fourth quarter of 2025 and further escalating to over 9% in the first quarter of 2026, thus far, with only a week remaining in the quarter, and profitability far exceeded the prior year in the fourth quarter of 2025.”
  • Christina emphasized the rollout of the company’s most comprehensive new menu in its history and the successful launch of craveable limited time offers, including Chili Garlic Ramen and Steak Stroganoff, which have attracted new customer groups and driven engagement.
  • The company undertook a portfolio review, closing underperforming restaurants. Christina stated this “resulted in a material transfer of sales to nearby locations, resulting in a step baseline increase of average sales volume at those go-forward restaurants, which also favorably impacted margins.”
  • Operational improvements were achieved through the Operational Excellence Review (OER) program, raising standards and accountability, leading to improved guest satisfaction scores and operational consistency.
  • Christina reported that marketing efforts shifted to a more data-driven, AI-supported approach, allowing for dynamic adjustment of investments and improved targeting, which reinforced brand engagement.
  • CFO Michael Hynes said, “In the fourth quarter, our total revenue increased 0.8% compared to last year to $122.8 million...System-wide comp restaurant sales during the fourth quarter increased 6.6% including an increase of 7.3% at company-owned restaurants and an increase of 3.8% at franchised restaurants.”

Outlook

  • The company’s forecast for Q1 2026 projects comp sales of approximately 9% and adjusted EBITDA of $5.7 million to $6.3 million.
  • For the full year 2026, guidance includes: “Total revenue of $478 million to $493 million, including comp restaurant sales growth of 6% to 9%. Restaurant contribution margin between 14.7% and 16%; general and administrative expenses of $49 million to $52 million...Depreciation and amortization expense of $24 million to $25 million; interest expense of $10 million to $11 million; adjusted EBITDA between $30 million and $35 million, 1 to 2 new franchise restaurant openings and we estimate total 2026 capital expenditures of $9.5 million to $10.5 million.”
  • Hynes added, “We expect to be free cash flow positive and have the opportunity to reduce our debt balance in 2026 by $5 million to $10 million.”

Financial Results

  • Hynes reported, “Company average unit volumes in the fourth quarter increased 9.9% to $1.44 million.”
  • Restaurant contribution margin in Q4 2025 was 14.1%, an increase of 290 basis points year-over-year.
  • Cost of goods sold in Q4 was 26.0% of sales, down 120 basis points year-over-year, driven by menu price, vendor rebates, and lower discounting, partially offset by higher food costs and modest inflation.
  • Labor costs for Q4 were 30.9% of sales, down 140 basis points due to sales leverage, despite 2.3% hourly wage inflation.
  • Net loss for Q4 was $6.8 million or a loss of $1.16 per diluted share, including a $5.6 million noncash impairment charge related to restaurant closures.
  • Adjusted EBITDA in Q4 was $7.6 million compared to $4 million a year ago.
  • The company closed 9 company-owned and 3 franchise restaurants during the quarter, totaling 33 closures in 2025 and 20 year-to-date in 2026, benefiting comp sales by an estimated 100 to 150 basis points in Q4.

Q&A

  • Todd Brooks, The Benchmark Company: Asked about the contribution of sales transfer from closures to the improvement in 2026 guidance. Hynes responded, “If we look at the full year guidance for '26, $30 million to $35 million of adjusted EBITDA...a little less than half of that is -- will be due to closures just under $5 million with the rest due to core business improvement.”
  • Brooks also inquired about the impact of tax refunds and macro factors on the 9% comp sales figure. Christina noted, “We see a lot of consistency. It's not like we saw a big change in March when tax refunds would have started coming in or post the conflict...industry has been hovering at the 0% to 1% same-store sales, and we've been consistently meeting that, going back to early '26 by over 9 percentage points.”
  • Brooks queried about the Delicious Duos mix. Christina replied, “right around 5%, which is what we expect it to be since its inception back in late July last year.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts expressed keen interest in the sustainability and drivers of the current sales momentum, with a generally positive tone and congratulatory remarks on recent performance.
  • Management maintained a confident tone in prepared remarks, emphasizing strong operational execution and sales acceleration; during Q&A, responses were direct and factual, with Hynes quantifying the sources of EBITDA improvement and Christina noting consistency in trends despite macro factors.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, both analyst and management sentiment remained optimistic, but with increased confidence and specificity regarding the drivers of ongoing improvement.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for 2026 is more robust, projecting higher comp sales growth (6% to 9%) and improved restaurant margins (14.7% to 16%) compared to previous guidance for 2025.
  • Management’s tone shifted from cautious optimism in Q3 2025 to a more assertive confidence in Q4, supported by strong Q4 results and early Q1 2026 performance.
  • The focus on portfolio optimization and operational excellence has intensified, with greater detail provided on the quantitative impact of restaurant closures and sales transfer.
  • Analysts’ focus progressed from value platform and LTO success in Q3 to more granular questions regarding margin and sales transfer in Q4.

Risks and Concerns

  • Christina referenced the ongoing review of strategic alternatives, emphasizing, “No decisions have yet been made, and there is no set timetable for completion. Until the review is completed, we will not provide additional commentary.”
  • Hynes highlighted modest food inflation and wage inflation as ongoing cost pressures, though these were partially mitigated by pricing and sales leverage.
  • The company continues to monitor the macroeconomic environment for consumer value sensitivities, responding with menu pricing and value initiatives like Delicious Duos.

Final Takeaway

Management underscored that the foundation built in 2025, marked by operational discipline, targeted marketing, and portfolio optimization, is delivering tangible financial improvement and positioning Noodles & Company for sustained growth. The company expects continued margin expansion, robust comp sales, and positive free cash flow in 2026, with a clear commitment to optimizing the restaurant portfolio and strengthening guest engagement through menu innovation and value-driven offerings.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: EPA issues waiver to allow E15 gasoline in bid to ease pump prices
data.description:
The Environmental Protection Agency issued a temporary waiver Wednesday to allow the nationwide sale of E15 ethanol gasoline, as part of an effort to maintain domestic supplies and ease prices at the gasoline pump that have surged because of war in the Middle East.

The waiver, which takes effect for 20 days starting May ⁠1 and can be extended if needed, will allow retailers to sell less expensive formulations of gasoline, including mixtures that include ​15% ethanol, that are typically not permitted during ​warmer months.

The EPA's action also will remove all federal impediments to selling E10—gasoline blended with 10% ethanol—across the country, including in a group of seven Midwestern states that would have otherwise required that blend to be mixed with less volatile but costlier blendstocks.

Analysts ‌said ⁠the moves could trim several cents per gallon off retail prices; the U.S. average price for regular gasoline is running just over $3.98/gal, up more than a dollar from a month ago, according to AAA.

"We foresee potential ​for a disruption to the American fuel supply," EPA Administrator Zeldin said ​at a press conference at the CERAWeek energy conference in Houston, where the waiver was announced.

Potential relevant stocks include Valero Energy (VLO), Marathon Petroleum (MPC), Phillips 66 (PSX), HF Sinclair (DINO), PBF Energy (PBF), Delek US (DK), Archer Daniels Midland (ADM), Bunge (BG), Green Plains (GPRE), Gevo (GEVO), Clean Energy Fuels (CLNE), REX American Resources (REX), Darling Ingredients (DAR), FutureFuel (FF).

ETF: (CRAK)

data.title: Precigen expects Q1 revenue to exceed $18M as PAPZIMEOS launch accelerates with broad U.S. payer coverage
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Precigen, Inc. (PGEN) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Helen Sabzevari, President and CEO, highlighted the company's transformation to a "product revenue-generating commercial biotech company" following the full FDA approval and commercial launch of PAPZIMEOS, the first therapeutic for adult recurrent respiratory papillomatosis (RRP). She reported, "net product revenue for Q4 2025 was $3.4 million, with shipments commencing in November" and stated, "we expect revenues in Q1 to exceed $18 million."
  • Sabzevari emphasized PAPZIMEOS' "broad label for adult RRP with no restriction based on the number of prior surgeries" and underscored its "unmatched efficacy" and "durable ongoing responses," citing more than three years of follow-up in clinical studies. She noted a consensus paper published by 16 leading U.S. physicians recommending PAPZIMEOS as the "preferred first-line treatment for adults with recurrent respiratory papillomatosis."
  • Phil Tennant, Chief Commercial Officer, described the rapid expansion of payer coverage, stating, "In early January, we had approximately 170 million lives covered, which has now increased to approximately 215 million, including nearly all major payers across commercial, Medicare and Medicaid." Tennant reported over 300 patients in the Precigen patient support hub and observed, "Brand utilization is accelerating across the country in both the large institutions and academic centers as well as in the community setting."
  • Harry Thomasian, Chief Financial Officer, stated, "Revenue for the year totaled $9.7 million versus $3.9 million in 2024, resulting in an increase of $5.8 million or 149%." He added, "We expect revenue for Q1 of 2026 will exceed $18 million." Thomasian also noted, "Our net loss attributable to common shareholders was $429.6 million, or $1.37 per share for the year ended December 31, 2025," with two large noncash items totaling $318.5 million or $1.02 per share, which "will not recur in the future."
  • Sabzevari announced plans to begin a PAPZIMEOS clinical trial in pediatric RRP in Q4 2026 and referenced efforts for geographic expansion, including a marketing authorization application to the EMA.

Outlook

  • Sabzevari and Thomasian both projected Q1 2026 revenue to exceed $18 million, driven solely by PAPZIMEOS product sales. Tennant indicated, "We expect these trends to continue, assisted by the assignment of the permanent J-code from April 1 and supported by the continued durability of response that we are seeing in patients."
  • No regular revenue guidance is planned going forward, with a focus on indicators for long-term launch trajectory.
  • Management expects continued institutional activation and utilization within community practices, with further progress updates anticipated in the next quarter.

Financial Results

  • Q4 2025 net product revenue from PAPZIMEOS was $3.4 million, reflecting its commercial launch in November. Full-year 2025 revenue reached $9.7 million, a 149% increase compared to 2024.
  • Research and development expenses decreased by $11.7 million, or 22.1%, primarily due to "strategic prioritization of the company's pipeline announced in 2024."
  • Selling, general and administrative expenses increased by $28.8 million, or 69.8%, mainly due to "a $27.3 million increase in costs incurred related to PAPZIMEOS' commercial activities."
  • The company ended 2025 with $100.4 million in cash, cash equivalents and investments, with expectations to reach cash flow breakeven by the end of 2026.
  • Net loss attributable to common shareholders was $429.6 million, or $1.37 per share, including nonrecurring noncash items totaling $318.5 million.

Q&A

  • Jason Butler, Citizens JMP Securities: Asked about patient flow from the hub to reimbursed treatment and the timing of second doses. Phil Tennant responded, "We want to make sure that the vast majority, if not all of those patients are converted on to treatment," noting conversion timing varies by institution. Sabzevari added, "Patients are moving through all their treatments and some...have moved through their last treatment."
  • Swayampakula Ramakanth, H.C. Wainwright: Inquired about the impact of the J-code on patient onboarding. Tennant replied, "The J-code really does simplify the workflow and billing process...it increases certainty and, of course, speed at which these patients should be processed."
  • Ramakanth also asked about European regulatory progress. Sabzevari confirmed the EMA application is under review and that Precigen is receiving "tremendous enthusiasm from the physicians really looking forward to having this first line and a standard of care therapy."
  • Brian Cheng, JPMorgan: Sought clarification on whether the $18 million revenue guidance includes only PAPZIMEOS product revenue. Thomasian stated, "Yes, that $18 million...includes all the PAPZIMEOS. No other revenue." Cheng also asked about stocking effects and prescriber numbers. Tennant said, "There is very little stocking...the number of prescribers is increasing."
  • Michael DiFiore, Evercore ISI: Asked about repeat prescribers in community sites. Tennant noted, "We actually think the community is going to be a significant contributor to our overall business as we go forward."
  • DiFiore also asked about payer mix and gross-to-net. Tennant reported, "about 60% to 65% commercial," and Thomasian guided that "gross to net will be in the high teens, low 20s."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts expressed enthusiasm for the strong launch trajectory but focused on operational details, conversion rates, the J-code's impact, and expansion plans, maintaining a slightly positive but probing tone.
  • Management was confident and optimistic throughout, highlighting "strong momentum," "phenomenal progress," and "tremendous progress on the PAPZIMEOS commercial launch." Sabzevari underscored, "we are making tremendous progress," and Tennant spoke about being "very excited by the response."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, both analysts and management displayed increased confidence, with management shifting from cautious optimism to assertive projections, and analysts moving from basic operational questions to more nuanced inquiries on expansion and revenue composition.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Q4 marked a shift from early commercial groundwork in Q3 to rapid revenue growth and acceleration of patient onboarding in Q4, with Q1 2026 revenue projected to exceed $18 million versus no specific guidance previously.
  • Management confidence increased, with more assertive forward-looking statements and emphasis on broad market adoption, payer coverage, and imminent cash flow breakeven.
  • Analysts in Q4 focused more on operational execution, conversion rates, and international expansion, whereas Q3 questions centered on launch readiness and initial patient onboarding.
  • Key metrics such as revenue, patient support hub numbers, and insured lives covered showed significant growth from Q3 to Q4.

Risks and Concerns

  • The company acknowledged that the Q1 revenue projection is a one-time update due to timing, indicating no intention for regular revenue guidance.
  • Management cited the importance of payer coverage and institutional activation as potential hurdles but presented the permanent J-code as a solution to streamline patient access.
  • Expansion into pediatric indications and geographic markets is underway but remains subject to regulatory review and operational execution.

Final Takeaway

Precigen management reiterated that the commercial launch of PAPZIMEOS has driven a rapid transformation into a revenue-generating company, with Q1 2026 revenue expected to exceed $18 million and broad payer coverage now in place. The company highlighted continued momentum in prescriber and patient onboarding, as well as strategic plans for pediatric trials and European expansion, aiming for cash flow breakeven by the end of 2026.
